description: Massive orchestral epic with soaring choir, thundering percussion, and heroic triumph — perfect for trailers and climactic moments
suno_tags: epic cinematic, orchestral, dramatic choir, thundering percussion, soaring strings, powerful, heroic, 80 BPM
vocal_style: Operatic and commanding for chorus sections. Solo voice in verses — powerful but restrained, building anticipation. Full choir erupts in choruses with layered harmonies. The voice should feel ancient, timeless, larger than human.

tips: Write in the language of myth — rise, fall, kingdoms, fire, dawn
Chorus should feel like a declaration carved in stone
Verses set the scene, choruses declare the truth
Bridge should be the darkest moment before the triumphant final chorus

example_lyrics:
[Intro]
[Timpani]
[Instrumental]

[Verse 1]
[Soft]
From the ashes of forgotten thrones
Where silence kept its watch alone
A ember stirred beneath the stone
A voice the darkness should have known

[Pre-Chorus]
[Energy: Building]
The ground begins to shake and crack
There is no turning back

[Chorus]
[Choir: Gospel]
[Powerful]
Rise, rise from the ruin
Let the heavens hear your name
Born of fire, forged in thunder
Nothing holds us down again

[Interlude]
[Instrumental]
[Cello]

[Verse 2]
Across the fields of broken swords
Where heroes fell without a word
The banner flies against the storm
A thousand voices, one accord

[Pre-Chorus]
[Energy: Building]
The walls of night begin to fall
We answer the call

[Chorus]
[Choir: Gospel]
[Belted]
Rise, rise from the ruin
Let the heavens hear your name
Born of fire, forged in thunder
Nothing holds us down again

[Bridge]
[Whispered]
Even stars must fall to darkness
Before they learn to burn
[Timpani]
[Energy: Building]
And from the deepest night
We return

[Chorus]
[Powerful]
Rise, rise from the ruin
Let the heavens hear your name
Born of fire, forged in thunder
Nothing holds us down again

[Outro]
[Choir: Gospel]
[Instrumental]
[End]
do: Build from minimal to massive — the arc IS the experience
Use timpani, strings, and choir as the three pillars of sound
Write lyrics that feel timeless — they could be from any era
Include an instrumental interlude to let the orchestration breathe
Make the bridge a whispered moment of doubt before the triumphant finale
dont: Don't use modern slang or casual language — this is mythic territory
Don't keep the dynamics flat — cinematic music lives on contrast
Don't write more than 4 lines per verse — space lets the orchestra fill
Don't skip the choir in the chorus — it's the genre's defining element
Don't end quietly — epic cinematic songs finish with full orchestral power
